narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 68.8% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, and background details like your upper-middle family income and plans to attend full-time. The model's judgment hinged most strongly on your Year 1 grade decile of 4, which falls well below the average of 6 for students who passed the bar, signaling to the model a foundational academic struggle that strongly pushed the prediction toward failure. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 35, lower than the 37.27 average among bar passers, further tilting the odds against you by highlighting a gap in standardized reasoning skills key to legal success. Your Year 3 decile of 5, close to the 5.91 average for passers, offered some counterbalance, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing and showing later improvement. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 exceeds the 3.24 average for those who passed, and your full-time status aligns with most successful students, these weren't enough to offset the top concerns. Compared to typical admitted students, who often enter with stronger early grades and higher LSATs, your profile appeared riskier for bar passage despite positives like your solid later performance and family resources. This isn't a reflection of your potential or drive—many with similar starts have succeeded through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, as even a modest gain could shift future predictions favorably. Strengthening your early academic foundation through prep courses or additional undergrad-level work might also help demonstrate growth. Reapplying after addressing these areas, perhaps with updated transcripts showing continued Year 3-level progress, positions you well for reconsideration. You're already showing upward momentum; building on that strategically can open doors.
